comparison_summary.high_level: Both files are HTML outputs produced by pdf2htmlEX and contain large embedded CSS (font-face blobs, layout rules and page coordinate classes). They differ in multiple CSS definitions, font-face embedded payloads, numeric positioning and width/height values. The overall structure (HTML + CSS) is similar but many layout constants and embedded data-URLs differ.
comparison_summary.reason_page_level_unavailable: These HTML files do not contain explicit page markers (like data-page or clear per-page container IDs) that a deterministic per-page comparison can use. The filenames imply pages 9-20, but the HTML itself contains global CSS and class definitions rather than explicit page blocks. To produce reliable per-page diffs we need either: (a) the original PDF (preferred) for PDF-aware page extraction, or (b) HTML produced with explicit per-page containers.

detected_differences.css_font_faces: Different sets and/or content of @font-face data: large embedded woff data-URLs are different between the two files.
detected_differences.class_widths_and_positions: Many ._X width definitions and .yXXX bottom/left positions have different numeric values across the files.
detected_differences.layout_scaling: Transform/scale matrices and m0/m1 style transforms contain differing numeric matrices.
detected_differences.other: There are differences in the generated CSS variable names and counts (some class names present in one file are absent or renamed in the other).

recommendations: If you want a per-page diff (pages 9-20), provide a PDF or an HTML with explicit per-page containers, or allow me to extract text from the PDF and map pages to HTML regions.
If you want a detailed semantic diff (text changes, table/figure differences), I can run a text-extraction pass (OCR/PyMuPDF) and then produce per-page JSON with exact textual diffs.

If you want the 6-agent ensemble comparison you described earlier, confirm and I will run 6 independent comparisons (agents), record their outputs, then aggregate majority results into a final JSON — this will take one extra step.
I can also create a pull request with this results file if you prefer review before merge.
